title: SEGMENT 7 — The bystander effect — T-DXd's leak-and-kill superpower

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — The bystander effect — T-DXd's leak-and-kill superpower

I keep using this phrase, the bystander effect, and now it's time to slow down and explain exactly what it is, because it's the single most important concept that distinguishes the second generation of ADCs from the first, and it's the reason T-DXd is rewriting clinical practice.
Here's the analogy.
Imagine you're back to bombing that enemy command bunker in the middle of a friendly city.
Your guided missile works — it flies through the city, finds the bunker, detonates inside.
But the bunker is part of a complex.
There are tunnels underneath, ammo dumps next door, a fuel depot down the street.
None of those secondary targets are emitting the radio signal that your missile's targeting computer is locked onto.
From the missile's perspective, they're invisible.
They get a free pass.
Now imagine you upgrade the warhead.

Instead of a contained explosion, the warhead is a chemical agent — a vapor — that, when released inside the bunker, leaks out through the air vents, drifts through the tunnels, seeps into the ammo dump, fills the fuel depot.
It doesn't need to be specifically targeted at those secondary structures, because it diffuses there on its own.
One missile, one detonation, but the killing radius is now much larger than just the bunker.
That's the bystander effect.
Mechanically, here's what's happening.
A T-DXd molecule binds to HER2 on a cancer cell.
The HER2-positive cell internalizes the ADC.
Inside the lysosome, the cleavable linker is cut, and free DXd — the topoisomerase I inhibitor payload — is released inside the cell.
DXd is a small molecule, only about five hundred daltons, with no permanent charges.
That combination of small size and neutral charge means DXd can pass through cellular membranes by simple diffusion.
It does not need a transporter.
It does not need an active uptake mechanism.
It just leaks.
So some of the DXd does its job in the cell that internalized it — it inhibits topoisomerase I, generates DNA damage, kills the cell.
But some of the DXd leaks out across the plasma membrane and into the extracellular space, where it encounters neighboring cells.
Those neighboring cells take it up — again by diffusion, no special machinery required.
And in those neighbors, DXd does the same thing it did in the original cell.
It inhibits topo I.
It generates DNA damage.
It kills them.
Regardless of whether they express HER2.
This is the bystander effect.
The killing radius of the drug is no longer limited to the cells the antibody can directly bind.
It extends to the neighbors, the bystanders, the cells in the tumor microenvironment that happen to be sitting next to a HER2-positive cell.
And in a heterogeneous tumor, where some cells are HER2-high and others are HER2-low or HER2-negative, the bystander effect dramatically expands the population of cells you can kill.
T-DM1, by contrast, has essentially no bystander effect.
Remember from the last segment that T-DM1 uses a non-cleavable linker, so the released payload is lysine-MCC-DM1 — a charged molecule that doesn't cross membranes well.
Once it's released inside a cell, it tends to stay there.
It kills the cell that internalized the drug, and only that cell.
Neighbors are safe.
That's why T-DM1 doesn't work in HER2-low disease and T-DXd does.
In HER2-low tumors, only a fraction of the cells express enough HER2 to internalize meaningful amounts of drug.
With T-DM1, the rest of the tumor is essentially untouched.
With T-DXd, the HER2-positive cells act as delivery beacons — they pull in the ADC, release the payload, and the payload then diffuses out to kill the surrounding HER2-low cells.
The whole tumor gets hit, even though only some of it expresses the target.
You can see why this is both a superpower and a vulnerability.
The superpower is obvious — expanded killing radius, applicability to heterogeneous tumors, efficacy in low-antigen patient populations.
The vulnerability is that the bystander effect doesn't stop at the tumor edge.
Once payload starts leaking, it can leak from circulating drug too, or from drug that's been taken up by non-tumor tissues.
Healthy cells in the vicinity of any HER2-expressing tissue can be exposed to the released DXd.
That's part of why T-DXd has a different toxicity profile than T-DM1 — particularly the interstitial lung disease that we'll talk about in Segment 15.
Here's another way to think about it.
A non-bystander ADC like T-DM1 is a sniper rifle.
Every shot hits exactly the cell that bears the target.
Clean.
Precise.
But limited to what the sniper can see.
A bystander-capable ADC like T-DXd is a sniper rifle with a small cloud of nerve agent attached to each bullet.
The bullet hits the cell, and then a cloud of poison spreads out from the impact site, killing everything nearby.
More effective against dispersed enemies hiding among the visible ones.
More collateral damage when the cloud drifts where it shouldn't.
For an ADC designer, the bystander effect is the central trade-off.
If you design your payload to be highly membrane-permeable, you get strong bystander killing but you also get more systemic toxicity from any payload that leaks during circulation.
If you design your payload to be membrane-impermeable, you get clean targeting but you lose the ability to hit antigen-low cells.
Most modern ADC programs now deliberately design for bystander activity, on the bet that the clinical benefit outweighs the toxicity.
T-DXd has been the proof point for that bet.
One more thing worth saying.
The bystander effect is not unique to T-DXd.
Several other ADCs are designed for it — sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy), which we'll discuss in Segment 13, is also bystander-capable.
Brentuximab vedotin (Adcetris) is partially bystander-capable, because MMAE is somewhat membrane-permeable.
The bystander effect is now a feature you can choose to design in or out.
T-DXd just happens to be the most successful example of designing it in.
